For most of my life, I’ve been one of the ‘only women in the room’.
I was one of only eight women in my MBA class. I was one of the few female venture capitalists in the 90s, pumping
breast milk in a storage closet!
I am one of a small group of female financial copywriters. I’m one of a handful of women who write about options
trading. And my agency is part of a small but growing group of female-owned direct response agencies. I’ve survived
and even thrived in male-dominated industries for years. Then one day, everything changed…
I was attending yet another industry conference surrounded by men. Smart, talented, successful men. Lots and lots
of men. And I finally had to admit, it wasn’t enough. The men were great, but I missed the women…
I wanted more women in my life. I wanted more female mentors, colleagues, and collaborators. I wanted to
surround myself with more women. Smart, talented, successful women.
Because women know how it feels to suffer through the condescending ‘mansplaining’, inappropriate jokes, and
endless sports metaphors, without batting an eye. They understand what it’s like to be mommy-tracked or trapped
in a business meeting with breast milk leaking all over your blouse. And they know what it really takes to smash
through glass ceilings, because they’ve done it. So I began searching for all the women in the room. I grabbed every
one I could find and invited them to a small intimate dinner at the hotel that night.
We ended up sitting around the table for hours, sharing the truth of our lives, both personal and professional. We
swapped war stories, shared career advice, celebrated our successes, and toasted our failures. That was one of the
most memorable nights of my life. I had finally found the tribe of female friends and mentors I had been searching
for.
That was over five years ago. Today our tribe has grown into 670 incredible women known as the Titanides.
Together, the Titanides offer advice, mentoring, creative feedback, high fives, a kick in the butt, and a good laugh
when you need it. Most of all, these women have helped catapult each others’ careers to the next level…
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding
as a Woman in Business
By Marcella Allison,
Founder of the ‘Not-So-Secret’ Order of the Titanides
titanides.com
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 12 --

Since that first Titanides dinner, I’ve nearly tripled my income and solidified my reputation as an authority in my field.
And I’m not the only one…
New research in the Harvard Business Review reveals that women need women-only networks in order to succeed
at the highest levels in business. In fact, women who have BOTH a central network of well-connected peers… AND a
second women-only inner circle… land jobs with more authority and 250% BETTER pay!
In contrast, those women who are super connected but do NOT have a separate female-only network end up with the
least authority and the lowest pay. The researchers aren’t sure why… but they suspect that women pass valuable
information to each other just like old boy networks have done for years.
Look, the data is clear. Even if you have the exact same qualifications, education, and work experience as a man… you need
an ‘old girls club’ like the Titanides to succeed. I call it my dirty little sexist secret to succeeding as a woman in business.
Now I want to share that secret with you.
5 Keys to Success Every Woman In Business MUST Know
Here are the 5 critical lessons I’ve learned from leading the Titanides over the past 5 years. These are the secrets
that have allowed me to nearly triple my business and grow my reputation – all while having a ridiculous amount of
fun along the way.
These lessons are all backed by solid research. Because, as the networking study shows, there are some very clear
and measurable differences between what it takes for men and women to succeed at the highest levels.
Take a minute to read each lesson, then grab a pen and score yourself to see how you’re doing.
You may find, like I did, that you could benefit from more women in your life to hold you up, cheer you on, point you
in the right direction, connect you to others, drown your losses, and celebrate your wins.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business
2018 Gala Attendees
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 12 --

LESSON 1 - CHARGE WHAT YOU ARE WORTH
It may seem obvious.
But if you really want to succeed as a woman in business, you have to charge what you are worth.
Unfortunately, the data shows that many of us are still not being fairly compensated. Today, women of every race
are still paid less than men, at every education level. And the more experience you have, the wider the pay gap.
You’ve heard that infamous statistic that women earn just $0.80 on the dollar. But let’s stop and do the math.
In 2015, men working full-time earned a median annual salary of a little over $50,000, while women earned just
over $40,000 a year. That’s a loss of over $10,000 in median income every single year. Over a 40-year career,
that’s more than $400,000.
So you have to work an extra decade just to catch up!
And black and Hispanic women face an even bigger gap. They have to work more than two decades past retirement
just to close the gap. Which means that, unless you’re a vampire, you’ll never catch up. Now, you’d think that women
working in their own businesses would close the pay gap. After all, when you own your own business, you can set
your own price tag. Surely, we wouldn’t sabotage ourselves? Right? Wrong!
FreshBooks, a Canadian accounting software company, did the math. They compared invoices from male and female
entrepreneurs. And they made a shocking discovery…
It turns out that, on average, women charge their clients 28% LESS than men do. 28%!
That’s WORSE than eighty cents on the dollar! We are literally sabotaging ourselves.
If we want things to change, we can’t sit around waiting for a knight on a white horse to ride to the rescue. We have
to be our own knight, take charge, and break ourselves out of this lower pay prison.
So where do we begin? How do we ask for that extra 20 cents?
Well, first of all, we need to find the appropriate rate for the job. We need to check with someone else before we
agree to that price or send off that invoice.
When I first got into business as a copywriter, I was lucky enough to land a big gig with a top company. It was my
first time taking on this kind of project.
I had no idea what to charge. It could have been $10 or $10,000. I had no idea.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:
titanides.com
www.getwsodo.com	www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 12 --

So I contacted fellow Titanide, Monica Day, and asked her to take a look at my bid. Her response shocked me.
She told me, “If you seriously don’t increase your fees, I’m going to get on a plane and fly to Cincinnati and
personally beat you…”
I had no doubt she would do it. She knew the going rate for the work and she knew I was way underbidding it.
So do a buddy check before you send out that bid or that invoice. Check it with someone who knows the going rate for
your level of experience, because rates change as your experience changes. If you don’t have anyone who can help, con-
sider joining a networking group like the Titanides or searching for a pricing guide to get standard rates.
No matter what option you choose, do your homework.
Don’t guess and don’t underestimate yourself.
When in doubt, you can also check your prices with a man.
I was recently bidding on a project with a new client, and I asked fellow copywriter David Deutsch to take a look at the
bid for me because he had worked with this client before. And David called me back and said, “Double it.”
I said, “What?” He said, “Double it. Oh, and I’d increase the royalties too.”
So I doubled the bid. And I sent it out to the client. And guess what? No pushback, none, just done. He accepted the
full flat fee. We negotiated the royalty a bit and that was it. Overnight, I doubled my fee because I charged what I am
worth. Because I charged like a man, and I billed like a man, and I priced myself like a man.
If you want to succeed as a woman in business,
you must charge what you are worth.

-- 4 of 12 --

The second thing you have to do if you want to succeed as a woman in business is stop carrying the mental load!
What do I mean by the mental load? It’s the organization and planning and directing all the activity that has to get done
for the entire household, even if you are not actually doing all the work yourself.
Here’s an example: Let’s say your partner offers to cook dinner. This is great, right?
But if you send 10 text messages saying, “So there’s spaghetti in the cupboard, there’s a jar of sauce, there’s some
hamburger in the refrigerator, but remember don’t put mushrooms in, because Johnny’s allergic to them, but if you
could make the spaghetti, and have that ready by 5 pm before we leave for soccer practice...” If you are planning
everything your partner is executing, you are carrying 100% of the mental load… even if they did boil the water.
And if you complain when they miss something and they say, “But you didn’t ask me to do that…” then that’s a clear
sign you are carrying the mental load! Bright Horizons, a chain of national childcare centers around the United States,
recently did a survey of working mothers. Unlike previous generations, our male partners now know where to find the
vacuum cleaner and how to change a diaper… but it turns out, we still have to tell them to do it!
Women are TWICE as likely to manage the business of the household, and THREE times as likely to manage
children’s schedules. But we don’t just carry the mental load at home with our partners and kids. We also do
this when we’re on projects with other people, when we end up acting like “the mom” in business situations.
I notice many times with my male clients, that I am the one carrying the mental load for the entire project.
I finished a project a year ago for an options trading service. At the end, I sat down and divided up the hours
I was actually writing or researching copy vs. the amount of time I was carrying the mental load for the project.
In other words, when I was basically acting as mom for the entire team.
Almost half of my project time was spent carrying the mental load. And just like at home, I wasn’t getting paid for that
extra time. So, I went back and re-negotiated my contract and they ended up hiring someone to do the job I had been
doing for free! If you want to succeed as a woman in business, you have to choose yourself.
You have to stop carrying all the mental load at home and at work.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:

-- 5 of 12 --

If you’re a female entrepreneur or freelancer, then you’re already way too familiar with the sacrifices necessary to build
your own business. But you might be surprised to learn that men and women sacrifice different things. And that differ-
ence could kill you… literally.
Bank of America does a survey of small business owners in the United States every couple of years. One of the questions
they ask is about personal sacrifice. It’s probably no surprise to you that more than seven out of ten small business owners
admit they’ve made significant personal sacrifices for their business. But when they asked business owners, “What have
you sacrificed?” there was a huge difference between men and women.
Men said, “Time with my wife and my kids.” Women said, “Time for myself and my social life.”
So let me ask you this question: How much time do you spend taking care of yourself vs. taking care of others?
Healthy Women and Working Mother Magazine recently asked women to rank the time they spent managing their own
health care vs. caring for others. The results were shocking… Turns out we take care of our children, our pets, our parents,
and our partners, BEFORE we ever take care of our own health. That’s right, we put our own health dead last. And that’s
ONLY if we get around to taking care of our own health at ALL. Nearly eight out of ten of us never get around to our own
health appointments at all because we’re too busy taking care of everyone else.
Fellow Titanide Jen Adams cracked me up when she told me her family doctor refused to book another appointment for
her children until Jen booked her own physical. I’m just as guilty. It’s way too easy to sit at my desk drinking coffee instead
of getting up and moving. And I’ve got the waistline to prove it! I book appointments for the dog before I book my own
checkup. And I’ve had “get a massage” on my to-do-list for weeks. But unless I start taking care of myself, I won’t have the
energy to fulfill my dreams. It takes stamina to build a business, physical stamina and emotional resilience.
Last year, fellow Titanide, Taru Nieminen, created a custom Titanides 30-Day Self Care Challenge to help us all love our-
selves a little more and a little better. Together we practiced getting more sleep, writing gratitude lists, cooking healthy
meals, enjoying a pedicure, walking, dancing, napping and more. Then we all posted and reported on how we were doing.
Somehow, it’s easier to give yourself permission to take care of you when it’s a group assignment!
You can’t succeed as a woman in business if you are not taking care of yourself.
And that means finding time for yourself and taking care of your own health.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:
LESSON 3 - PRACTICE SELF CARE FIRST

-- 6 of 12 --

All entrepreneurs and freelancers face an intimidating gauntlet of obstacles when trying to grow their business.
But again, the research shows, those obstacles are different for men and women.
Canadian researchers recently looked at all the obstacles entrepreneurs face in business building. They looked at all kinds
of things like business experience, access to capital, as well as things like sexism and negative effects on family life.
But the single biggest obstacle for women was none of these things.
The number one obstacle women faced was their own negative self-image.
We all have an inner critic or “mean girl.” We all have that nasty judgmental voice in our heads that bullies us and tells us
we’re not good enough. It’s time to put a muzzle on that voice.
That’s why in the Titanides, we have a rule that we lift up and encourage other women, we do not tear them down.
We help each other see and appreciate the greatness in all of us. Not only that, but we look for role models
everywhere, in history, in the news, and we celebrate these women as “honorary Titanides.”
Recently we celebrated Miss Nigeria at the Miss World pageant 2019. Not because she won, but because of how she
celebrated and cheered for Miss Jamaica, who beat her for the crown!
We honored, Leila Janah, the female entrepreneur who created digital jobs that pay a living wage to thousands in Africa
and India. And we toasted record-breaking American astronaut Christina Koch. By holding up a mirror to each other,
we can reflect back the best in all of us. We all deserve our own personal cheering section!
If you want to succeed as a woman in business, you have to first believe that you can.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:
LESSON 4 - BELIEVE IN YOUR OWN GREATNESS

-- 7 of 12 --

If you run your own business, you’re well aware that it involves risk. And most people think of entrepreneurs as risk
takers. But once again, there’s a difference between men and women in business…
The Journal of Entrepreneurship did a “meta analysis” where they looked at all of the research done over the years
on male vs. female entrepreneurs.
According to the analysis, women are far more risk-averse than men.
Many female entrepreneurs are terrified to fail… and our unwillingness to fail stunts our growth.
Because we are afraid to take risks, our businesses do not grow as quickly.
We don’t want to risk failing, so we play small, and as a result, we stay small.
Now this obviously isn’t true for every woman, but it was true for a remarkable number of women entrepreneurs.
One of the only ways to get over your fear of failure and take more risks is to practice failing more often. Think of it
like an inoculation.
So last year, fellow Titanide Brit McGinnis created the Titanides 100 Days of Failure Challenge, where we reported
daily on our failures both big and small. We gloriously failed at losing weight, launching new products, getting cli-
ents and more. Then we got back up and did it again.
The exercise was surprisingly freeing. It’s so much easier to risk when you don’t feel the tidal wave of
shame if you fail.
If you want to succeed as a woman in business, you’re going to have to get
comfortable taking bigger risks more often.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:
LESSON 5 - RISK MORE, MORE OFTEN

As you’ve gone through this special report, you’ve been scoring yourself in five categories.
As a quick reminder, list your score from 1-10 in the below categories:
. Charge What You Are Worth: Score ___
. Stop Carrying The Mental Load: Score ___
. Practice Self Care: Score ___
. Believe in Your Own Greatness: Score ___
. Risk More and Often: Score ___
Now, I want you to add up all your numbers. Take all five categories and write down your
total in your journal and circle it and put down today’s date.
No matter where you scored, you can work on these five areas for the next year, and you can
get those scores up.
You can close the gap between men and women when it comes to business success.
And I’m going to show you how to have more confidence and support as you do it.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:

-- 9 of 12 --

A few months ago, I was reading an article in the Harvard Business Review by Shawn Achor.
His book is called Big Potential. It’s about how the power of community can increase your chances of success.
In the book, he tells the story of how he had just spoken at a large women’s conference, and he was flying home.
He had the program out on his lap, and the guy next to him asked about it.
And when Shawn told him about the event, the guy said something dismissive like, “Does that crap really
make any difference?”
Shawn was so frustrated at his inability to coherently defend the need for women-only conferences and networking
events that he called another author, Michelle Gielan. They decided to study 2,600 women across all kinds of jobs and
industries, who had attended at least one conference for women.
They decided to measure two things. They wanted to know, if a woman attends a networking event, does it improve
her income? Does she in fact make more money?
And they also wanted to know if a woman who attends a woman’s networking event experiences any intangible benefits?
Is she less stressed, more confident, more connected?
They were hoping to show a modest improvement so they could defend themselves against jerks on airplanes.
But what they discovered blew their minds.
In the year after a woman attends a conference of women and connects with her peers, that woman has double
the chance of getting a promotion. Double!
Not only that, but she has triple the chance of earning 10% or more in the following year. Triple!
But it’s not just about the money. 8 out of 10 women who attend a women’s networking event report feeling
more optimistic about their future. And 7 out of 10 women report feeling more connected.
Now, research shows that when you feel more optimistic, you have better business outcomes.
And when you feel more connected, you are happier and more successful. So no wonder their income went up!
Now, here’s the thing. If you want these benefits (and those are some pretty awesome benefits in my opinion),
you have to take action.
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:
Why women need a room of our own to succeed

-- 10 of 12 --

Studies show that no matter how overqualified or over-prepared a woman may be, she often still hesitates to
take the leap. And we miss out on opportunities because of it.
Don’t hesitate to take the first step. Join the Titanides today. Find out what it’s like to have a tribe of talented,
generous, supportive women at your back.
You can count on us for a friendly shove, a shout of encouragement, or the confidence to jump when you need it.
Sometimes all it takes is a little nudge, and other times we need a big shove.
While social media may paint a happy picture, real life is different. The road to everything you desire will
never be a straight, smooth path. Life is going to twist and turn in unexpected ways.
You’re going to fall down. And that’s okay.
When the worst happens, Titanides get back up. We brush each other off, and help each other to make it across the
finish line. Every time.
Our community is a safe space where you can share the stories of your setbacks, defeats and all-is-lost moments,
without judgment or criticism. And when you succeed, we’ll be the first to pop the cork on the champagne!
And if you get stuck, or those setbacks seem like big boulders in the road instead of little pebbles you can kick out of
the way, you’ll have the resources you need to get around them.
We believe in freely sharing our resources and knowledge with each other. This could be anything from mentoring to
introducing a fellow member to a new contact or opportunity.
At some point, someone held a door open for you, and we all benefit when you open a door for the next woman.
Every woman has something to offer at every stage of the journey. If you’ve been in business for one month,
you have 30 days more experience than the woman who started today, so help a sister out!
You are not alone in entrepreneurship and business. Invest in yourself. Learn from the women who paved their own way.
Help other women who are behind you and benefit at the same time.
Take the first step, and put yourself first! Join today!
Welcome to the “Not-So-Secret” Order of the Titanides.
Marcella Allison
The Sexist Secret to Succeeding as a Woman in Business:
Take the first step.
P.S. Together the Titanides are building a new arena where dynamic, creative women co-mentor, elevate, and support each other on
the journey to success. Won’t you join us?

-- 11 of 12 --

As a copywriter, she currently works with some of the top direct-response companies
in the industry. Her clients include The Motley Fool, Bottom Line, Inc., Advanced
Bionutritionals, Money Map Press, Metabolic Living and more. Her copy has generated
over $100 million in sales for financial trading services, alternative health supplements,
and information products.
As a mentor and copy chief, she leads Money Map Press’s proprietary training program
for in-house and freelance copywriters. She also mentors over 600 women in the
Titanides.
In 2018, Marcella was awarded Copywriter of the Year by American Writers and Artists
Inc. (AWAI) for her outstanding performance record and impact on the copywriting
industry. She’s a regular featured speaker at industry events, including AWAI’s
Copywriting Success Bootcamp, Copy Chief LIVE, and The Copywriter Club In Real Life.
Marcella is the co-author of Why Didn’t Anyone Tell Me This Sh*t Before?, a collection
of wit and wisdom from women in business. As the founder of the Titanides, she is
dedicated to building a new arena, where dynamic, creative women co-mentor, elevate,
and support each other on the journey to success.

-- 12 of 12 --
